data.title: Arista Networks: Supply Constraints Limit Upside (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in September, I provided an update for Arista Networks Inc. (ANET). In that article, I reiterated my sell rating and expressed my view that a reality check could be coming as the outlook was soft and the valuation was rich. As you can see below, the stock has taken quite a tumble recently after earnings on Nov 4th, and so my thesis seems to have played out like I envisioned. Today, let's see if my rating needs updating after these events.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Arista Networks is overall well positioned as the data center switching market continues to expand. From their purchase commitments, you will see that demand isn't the problem. Instead, it seems that supply is dragging on growth, and forward guidance suggests these issues will persist into 2026. That said, the valuation has corrected, and so the stock now seems to be trading back at a reasonable valuation with regard to the long-term growth outlook. As a result, I'm upgrading to a hold.

Long Term Growth Story Is Intact

Firstly, despite my bearish take on the stock in my previous article, I would say that the company's long-term growth story remains highly intact. Even though there are recent worries about tech valuations, there is nothing to suggest that the trajectory of infrastructure buildout will slow. In the above charts, you can see the opportunity at hand.

For data center Ethernet switching, you can see that we are still in the very early innings, and you can see that the market could potentially hit $50 billion by 2029, a significant expansion from today's ~$30 billion market. As for campus Ethernet switching, forecasts also indicate notable expansion of the market from 2025 onwards for clients of all sizes, and so overall, from these two charts, you can see that Arista Networks is likely to continue benefitting from tailwinds in the next few years.

The above chart is a bit different and shows the data center ethernet switch market potential when excluding AI back-end networks. As you can see, moving forward, 800 Gbps switches will lead the way in terms of growth. Beyond that, 1.6 Tbps will become the bleeding edge. Therefore, in order to capture the growth of the data center switching market as a whole, Arista Networks will have to keep themselves at the forefront of the industry. So far, it seems they are succeeding.

Near the end of October, the company unveiled the new 800G R4 Series platform. Seamus Crehan, President of Crehan Research stated that "the 800GbE market is growing explosively, with port shipments more than tripling sequentially in Q2 '25, and Arista led in branded market share for both 800GbE as well as overall data center Ethernet switching." Therefore, it seems that Arista Networks currently holds both a technological and market share edge. Both factors should help to position the company well as infrastructure trends continue. Now let's take a look at their financial results for Q3.

Demand Is Strong

For Q3, Arista Networks reported revenues of $2.308 billion, up 27.5% YoY. While that's still a quite strong growth rate, it does represent a deceleration from the 30.4% increase seen in Q2. Even though this is definitely undesirable, it doesn't necessarily mean that demand for their products has weakened.

In the third quarter, the company recorded purchase commitments of $4.8 billion, double that of the prior year period, showing off just how strong demand is. The company stated that "purchase commitments remain elevated due to new product introductions," and so it should be clear that their technological leadership remains their competitive advantage.

Furthermore, it is reported that inventory turns improved from 1.3 to 1.4 YoY, and so clearly their products are not stuck on the shelf. At the end of the day, Arista Networks was able to beat analyst estimates for revenue by a fairly decent margin of $41.76 million, and so I would still consider their top-line results to be respectable.

Other Key Metrics

Strong Gross Margin

I discussed margins and profitability in my previous article as well, but for Q3, YoY gross margin expansion was even stronger than back in Q2. There was a 20 basis point expansion in the previous quarter, but for Q3, they managed a 60 basis point improvement. This suggests even better cost control and perhaps even more pricing power. Considering that demand is strong, as shown above, it wouldn't be surprising for Arista Networks to hold significant pricing power due to the technological competitiveness of their products.

Operating Margin Dipped

Despite good news for gross margin, operating margin dipped 50 basis points to 48.6%. In Q2 they saw notable expansion. However, investors shouldn't be too concerned at this point. Arista Networks stated that this weakness was "due to increased new product introduction costs" and so really there isn't anything wrong with the company's operations. It was mentioned above that new products are driving purchase commitments, and so these expenses are necessary for the long-term growth of the company.

Bottom Line Growth Also Slows

With operating margin coming in a bit weak, it shouldn't be a major surprise that bottom line growth underperformed when compared to top line growth. For EPS, Arista Networks reported an adjusted figure of $0.75, representing a 25% increase YoY. While they were still able to beat estimates by $0.04, there was a significant growth slowdown from Q2. Furthermore, according to consensus expectations, further slowdown is likely for the next few quarters, and so it is possible that some profitability weakness will continue. We'll take a look at their guidance next.

Near Term Outlook

For the fourth quarter, Arista Networks is expecting to see revenues of $2.35 billion at the midpoint, which means YoY growth of 21.8%. That would be another deceleration of top line growth from Q3. While demand is not a major concern at this stage according to the purchase commitments, further deceleration could start to be worrying as it may show that they aren't meeting demand in an efficient manner. For gross margin, they reported a non-GAAP figure of 64.2% in the year-ago quarter, and so cost control may weaken. However, in a reversal of fortunes, operating margin may shift back to expansion, as 47.0% was reported in 2024 Q4. Therefore, the increase in new product introduction expenses discussed earlier likely won't carry over.

A Softer 2026 Expected

In the EPS consensus expectations chart shown earlier, bottom-line growth was projected to continue moderating. That is due to three key factors, as shown by their preliminary 2026 guidance. For revenue growth, they expect just 20%, a slowdown from the expected full-year 2025 growth rate of between 26% and 27%. This either shows that demand will level off a bit or the company may struggle to keep up with demand. The latter seems more likely, but either way, continued slowdown could be a concern.

In addition to that, Arista Networks expects gross margin contraction at the midpoint due to "customer mix" and operating margin contraction due to continued investments in the business. While investments are not inherently a bad thing, they project a 400 basis point contraction from 2025 levels at the midpoint, and so the magnitude of deterioration may be a concern. As a whole, while purchase commitments are reassuring for demand, moving forward, the outlook seems a bit shaky in my view.

Valuation Slides

The forward P/E has corrected from 53.35 in my previous article to 43.00 and that potentially improves the risk/reward of the stock in my view. Firstly, for a good part of today's article, we discussed how the market is projected to expand in the next few years and how Arista Networks is well positioned. I keep going back to the purchase commitments figure because it shows that the company's growth is getting constrained by supply rather than the lack of demand. Therefore, despite the softer than desired Q4 and 2026 guidance, I would say that the company remains a robust business at its core.

I believe the sharp recent contraction in the valuation is a good reality check in that the near term isn't looking too rosy and now the stock is likely back to a fair valuation that accounts for the long term growth story. While the supply chain is not overly under their control, management should make this a key focus moving forward if they want to reaccelerate their financials.

Takeaway

Arista Networks is a leader that's well-positioned in the market. But at the same time, metrics point to supply constraints as their growth-limiting factor. With supply chains being so complex, there likely isn't an easy fix. The valuation has compensated, however, with the forward P/E ratio showing rather sharp contraction as of late. At this point, I view the valuation as a good demonstration of long-term opportunity along with near-term challenges, and so I have decided to upgrade to a hold rating.

data.title: Circle Internet Group: Share Price Now More Reflective Of Mounting Risks (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Circle's (CRCL) third-quarter results were strong, with efforts to diversify revenue beginning to pay off. This will only play out slowly, though, and in the meantime there is little to prevent a complete implosion of Circle's business in the event of a recession.

I previously highlighted the enormous risks Circle was facing, and its share price is down over 40% since then. Short-term interest rates continue to fall, although this has been fairly modest so far. Bitcoin's price is also down materially in recent weeks, which could temper demand for stablecoins. Despite this, I am now more neutral on Circle's stock after the recent decline, as the longer term narrative remains fairly compelling. Having said that, there will little to support Circle's share price if macro conditions continue to deteriorate.

Market Conditions

The most important recent development for stablecoins is the introduction of a far more favorable regulatory environment. While the full impact likely won't be felt for some time, Circle is best positioned to capitalize on this situation due to its focus on compliance.

Stablecoins in circulation increased 59% YoY in Q3, supported by surging markets. Circle’s market share also increased to 29% in the quarter. Stablecoin transaction volumes were reportedly up around 130% YoY, with USDC’s share growing to 40%. While this is positive, the recent market pullback is likely to temper demand for stablecoins in the short term. If this situation evolves into a bear market or recession, USDC's market capitalization could drop significantly, despite secular tailwinds.

Circle Business Updates

Circle's business continues to be dominated by the issuance of USDC, a US-dollar-denominated stablecoin. Circle also now offers the EURC stablecoin, but its market cap is still negligible. This is important as it means that Circle's revenue is predominantly dependent on short-term interest rates.

Circle is building financial infrastructure that extends well beyond just USDC, though. This includes the Arc layer-1 blockchain, which is tailored for stablecoins. Circle recently launched the Arc public testnet with more than 100 participants. While this is an interesting strategic move, it is not guaranteed to be successful. Arc faces fierce competition, and it is a late entrant into the market. This must be weighed against the fact that Circle has a distribution advantage and could use incentives to drive adoption. For example, Circle is already considering introducing a native token on the Arc Network.

Circle also offers CCTP (Cross-Chain Transfer Protocol), an infrastructure solution that enables the movement of USDC across blockchains, improving liquidity and the user experience. CCTP volume reached 31.3 billion USD in Q3, up 640% YoY. Circle has suggested that this represented around 47% of the bridged volumes that it tracks.

Circle also has the Circle Payments Network, a stablecoin payments solution targeted at financial institutions. It abstracts the complexity of the underlying blockchain system and enables fast, low-cost, and compliant global payments. CPN was only launched in May 2025 but is already seeing significant transaction volume growth. Support for 5 new chains was added in the quarter, and Circle continues to expand the CPN product portfolio. This includes CPN Console, a self-service solution for CPN members, and CPN Payouts, which enables automated stablecoin payouts on CPN. There are now 29 network members and a pipeline of 500 financial institutions waiting to join. CPN transaction volume is currently at around a 3.4 billion USD annual run rate.

The other important part of Circle's business at the moment is USYC, a tokenized money market fund. Circle acquired this product to support the rising use of yield-bearing digital assets as collateral in margin trading. USYC is now the second largest TMMF globally, with roughly 1 billion USD AUM.

Financial Analysis

USDC in circulation reached almost 74 billion USD in the third quarter, an increase of 108% YoY. Onchain transactions using USDC also grew 580% YoY to 9.6 trillion USD. Revenue and reserve income was only up 66% to 740 million USD though, with lower interest rates offsetting a substantial portion of the volume increase (return on reserves of ~4.15%). Transaction revenue totaled 4.7 million USD in the quarter. Other revenue was 29 million USD in Q3, up from less than 1 million USD in the prior year's comparable quarter. Subscription and services revenue totaled 23.6 million USD, which primarily relates to blockchain network partnerships.

Circle hasn't given specific near-term revenue guidance. USDC in circulation is expected to grow at a 40% CAGR over a multi-year period, though, which probably puts revenue growth at less than a 30% CAGR. Circle also now expects 90-100 million USD Other Revenue in 2025, which would mean that Other Revenue is fairly flat sequentially. Given the underlying trends in the business, this guidance appears overly conservative.

Total distribution and transaction and other costs increased 74% YoY in Q3 to 448 million USD. The increase was driven by distribution incentives and growth of USDC balances held on Coinbase's platform. As a result, Circle's RLDC margin declined to 39.5%.

Circle's adjusted operating expenses only increased 35% YoY to 131 million USD though, with adjusted EBITDA reaching 166 million USD at a 22% margin. While Circle has a highly profitable business, this would soon change if interest rates were to drop significantly.

Conclusion

I remain skeptical of Circle's business due to its current dependence on interest rates and cryptocurrency speculation. The company is moving in the right direction, though, with its infrastructure solutions and tokenized money market fund continuing to grow rapidly. It will likely be years before these alternative revenue sources are large enough to justify Circle's current market capitalization. In the meantime, weakening economic conditions continue to present a serious threat to Circle's share price. Despite this, I am now more neutral on Circle's stock, as it could soon find support after the recent decline.

data.title: McDonald's: A Resilient Defensive Play, But Premium Valuation May Cap Upside
data.description:
Following my last article on McDonald's (MCD), the stock price appreciated by 0.50%, yet underperformed the benchmark. This highlights my anticipation, following the thesis of an elevated multiple capping the upside in the near term.

My outlook hasn't changed. Although McDonald's remains a solid defensive play, which could help withstand market turbulence, MCD trades at a significant premium, clouding the future outlook. The forward P/E of 25x indicates a 55% premium versus the sector median of 16x and roughly 8% overvaluation compared to the average company in the S&P 500 at 23x earnings multiple. The iconic company deserves a premium multiple, but the current one may appear too inflated.

That's why I rate McDonald's a Hold. I still think the company may achieve the $329 price target over the next 12 months, indicating a 7% upside possibility, but it is unlikely to outperform the benchmark. I believe MCD is likely to follow bottom-line growth.

FQ3 2025: Double Miss Raises Red Flags

On November 5, the restaurant chain company delivered FQ3 2025 results. McDonald's posted a double-miss quarterly report, which raises red flags and indicates weakness in consumer spending. The market reacted to the results with a 2% decline over the following trading day.

McDonald's posted $7.08 billion in revenue, a 3% increase on a year-over-year basis, but $11 million below analysts' expectations. The bottom line arrived at $3.22 in diluted EPS, roughly flat on a year-over-year basis versus $3.23 in the same quarter last year, but $0.11 below what the market had anticipated. The company continues steady top-line growth, but deceleration in bottom-line growth may appear concerning, especially when MCD trades at a premium valuation to both the industry and the benchmark.

In the earnings release, McDonald's highlighted a positive tailwind, indicating steady operations across same-restaurant sales. The US market experienced a 2.4% increase on a year-over-year basis, mainly driven by positive check growth. The international markets surged by 4.3% over the past 12 months, primarily due to increased demand in Germany and Australia.

The international developmental licensed markets rose by 4.7% on a year-over-year basis, all geographic regions delivering positive growth, but led by comparable sales growth in Japan. I think this is important, indicating solid momentum in demand for the iconic brand and a significant market position globally, implying geographically well-diversified business operations.

I would also like to emphasize that franchised restaurants have led the top-line growth, suggesting a 7% increase on a year-over-year basis. What I think may be concerning is that sales by the company's owned and operated restaurants have decreased by 3%, arguing a weakening consumer and limited spending budgets. This may be concerning for long-term investors, and if comparable sales continue to erode, this could indicate a more structural issue for the company. I think investors should monitor dynamics further.

The company delivered $3.36 billion in operating income, a 5% increase on a year-over-year basis, and $2.28 billion in net income, reflecting a 1% growth over the past 12 months. These results support my bullish stance, representing a strong and mature business, but also struggling to accelerate revenue growth. Due to this, I argue that the market may not be willing to pay a higher premium for the company's stock.

To conclude, the company continues maintaining a dominant stance in the fast food industry, yet top and bottom line weakness argues for existing headwinds. I am favoring uncertainty around the macroeconomic outlook and weakening US consumer, rather than any structural issues, but investors should monitor the company's performance dynamics. If the economy enters a recession, sentiment could significantly shift, and the stock could correct from a premium valuation.

On the other hand, McDonald's remains well-diversified globally, including both the company's owned restaurants and licensed franchises. The company experiences steady sales growth in Germany, Australia, and Japan, which signals business continuation, yet I am not sure whether a solid low-single-digit revenue growth could be sufficient to justify multiple expansion.

Premium Valuation for Premium Business

I kindly invite you to read my previous article, which provides a more in-depth analysis of McDonald's valuations. However, I would also like to emphasize a few key things that support my thesis.

The chart below suggests that the current forward P/E of approximately 25 indicates a higher trading range over the past few years. This supports the thesis of limited upside, suggesting that investors are willing to pay a premium for robust business, yet I am not sure if stagnating performance supports the positive outlook.

One argument could be that MCD offers a defensive approach. The broader stock market has rallied since the April lows, and once volatility returns to the markets, McDonald's may appear to be a compelling opportunity to diversify into. However, the current valuation leaves little room for error, and that supports my neutral stance.

In addition to this, the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 10.20 and 8.20, respectively, argue for a bearish narrative, and that is another concern to account for. These valuation metrics imply an 8x overvaluation versus the sector medians of 1.29 and 0.90, which highlights that the market has likely already priced in the future revenue growth. At the current valuation ratios, the market knows that MCD is a stable and resilient business, but I don't think there is more room for multiple expansion. Even if the revenue growth resumes, these metrics argue that the stock price is not likely to appreciate, and it may need to unlock a new growth cycle. This stands for consolidation, which would result in the stock price mirroring the bottom-line performance.

The chart above indicates that MCD is used to trading at an elevated forward EV/Revenue level, but it also states a higher end of the trading range, signaling consolidation and capped upside in the near term.

McDonald's operates at substantial margins, and that is supporting a bullish narrative. The net income margin (TTM) of 32% suggests a 7x outperformance versus peers at 4.42%. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 57% reveals a 50% higher valuation versus the sector median of 38%. Although this argues for a bullish continuation, the company is heavily leveraged with nearly $56 billion in total obligations. Over the past 12 months, MCD paid $1.55 billion in interest expenses to service the debt, and that offsets the bullish outlook.

I kindly invite you to read my previous article, which indicates a more detailed valuation analysis. Nevertheless, McDonald's presents a mixed picture. The company is facing significant top and bottom line headwinds, but due to its iconic brand and mature business, this is likely a temporary hiccup. In the meantime, international business offsets the clouded comparable sales, and once the US consumer strengthens, top-line growth is likely to resume at a mid-single-digit pace.

MCD continues operating at substantial margins, which is supporting a bullish outlook, but it remains a heavily leveraged business with a significant financial burden. In addition to this, the company is not cheap, and valuation metrics indicate that even if the revenue growth resumes, the market may have already priced in the stock price, which may limit the upside in the near term. This supports my neutral stance on the restaurant chain company.

Wall Street estimates $12.14 in diluted EPS for FY2025 and $13.23 in diluted EPS for FY2026. I believe the company may be able to achieve these targets, supported by a strong presence in the operating fields, mature business flow, and significant margins. I also think that the stock is likely to mirror bottom-line growth, but I don't think MCD is well-positioned to outperform the benchmark. If we apply the current earnings multiple of 25x to the FY2026 EPS of $13.23, we arrive at my price target of $329.

Unlikely to Outperform the Benchmark

I think McDonald's may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking to diversify in the mature business, operating at an appealing margin, and having a significant presence in the space. The company also offers a 2.4% dividend yield, which may come as an attractive bonus to a defensive approach. MCD remains a stable and resilient business.

However, the risks and concerns remain, too, as I highlighted above and in my previous article. The company's premium valuation clouds the outlook, alongside stagnating top and bottom line growth, which could decline if the economy enters a recession. McDonald's remains heavily leveraged and operates in an industry full of fierce competition. If consumer habits change or people turn to healthier alternatives, structural issues may appear.

The clouded narrative supports my neutral stance. I rate McDonald's a Hold with a $329 price target. This argues for a 7% upside possibility without dividends but is unlikely to outperform the benchmark. 37 Wall Street analysts forecast a $331 price target, offering an 8% upside potential, and that is roughly in line with my estimate.

data.title: BUG: Increasing Exposure (Upgrade To Buy)
data.description:
Introduction

I have been a fan of the Cybersecurity sector for some time and consider it one of the key megatrends or a secular growth sector, one that does not suffer from economic volatility. These services are a must in the era of the Internet and now AI, where everything is interconnected and susceptible to attack and disruption that can cost far more than prevention. Last year, I analyzed the Global X Cybersecurity ETF (BUG) and found it to be a solid methodology, but at that point, expensive, and I reduced it to a Hold. I am now updating the view and upgrading to a Buy with 24% upside potential to YE26.

Portfolio Holding Changes

BUG tracks the INDXX Cybersecurity Index, which selects a maximum of 40 stocks from a wide array of subsectors and from companies that need to have at least 50% of revenue derived from cybersecurity. Given that the ETF has 24 stocks, it appears that the listed companies in the sector are scarce. The index also has a maximum weight of 6% per stock, which is based on market cap adjusted for free float and is rebalanced and reconstituted twice a year in February and November.

The current portfolio is slated to be rebalanced with Zscaler (ZS), CrowdStrike (CRWD), Palo Alto (PANW), and CyberArk (CYBR) reducing weights and distributing to others. Probably, CyberArk will not be eliminated until the merger with Palo Alto is approved, and thus, this should remain the largest de facto position until mid-2026.

The largest changes were the addition of SailPoint (SAIL) and Rubrick (RBRK), with almost 5% each. As seen in the performance section, this did not work well.

Performance

Despite excellent gains from the top four holdings in the ETF, the new additions, SailPoint and Rubrick, plus Fortinet (FTNT), dragged down the year-to-date performance.

Consensus upside of 24% to YE26

I gathered consensus price targets for 94% of the fund's AUM and calculated a weighted average upside of 24%, which is quite high vs what was calculated a year ago at around 9%. This could outperform the market and has upside risk when the ETF is rebalanced, as more weight may be added to Check Point (CHKP), SailPoint, etc., that have higher price targets vs. the top three. CyberArk will trade in line with Palo Alto and may add a few points unless the deal falls through.

Growth and Margin

Using consensus estimates for the ETF holdings, I calculated revenue growth of over 13% for 2026, which is almost half the rate that the top weight may grow at and is impacted by the more mature companies. Adjusted Net Income (adding back stock-based compensation) margins are expected to increase substantially from 14% to 20% by 2027 as many smaller companies attain scale and cross over into positive margins.

This combination of good revenue growth with expanding margins may drive adjusted EPS by 63% in 2026. Note that many companies are transitioning from losses to gains, and that influences the growth rate. For 2027, this earnings growth begins to normalize at around 24%, still high.

Valuation

Based on the consensus estimates, the BUG portfolio does not appear expensive on a relative or PEG basis, trading at 0.7x for the 2026-2027 period. While the top-tier stocks are over 2x PEG, a sizable portion of the smaller companies are cheaper due to very rapid earnings growth and margin expansion, as discussed earlier. The sector is scaling rapidly, with many companies transitioning to positive margins vs losses, which is a multi-year phenomenon.

Risk

The cybersecurity stocks are volatile and impacted by short-term earnings results that may not meet the market's expectations. We have seen many 10% to 20% sell-downs on poor guidance, as well as the occasional accident where a company's software did not meet up to standards. In addition, cyberattack complexity may be augmented with AI, which requires constant innovation and R&D for security companies, where failure can lead to significant business damage.

Conclusion

I rate BUG a Buy. The pure-play cybersecurity ETF will probably continue to be volatile, but the diversification should mitigate long-lasting damage. While the smaller companies offer exceptional upside as they transition to positive margins, they are highly susceptible to setbacks and failure. At this point, BUG offers a good balance of risk and reward.

data.title: Strata Critical Medical: Beginning A New Journey
data.description:
Today, I'm taking a look at Strata Critical Medical (SRTA), which until recently was known as Blade Air Mobility, which I last profiled in June 2024. Management recently made a key divestiture to change the company's business model, which necessitated the name change to Strata Critical Medical. The firm also picked up a new business as well. An updated analysis around this transformed company follows below.

Company Overview:

Leadership disposed of its Blade Passenger business to Joby Aviation (JOBY) for up to $125 million. This restructure made Strata a more focused company and a pure-play medical logistics company where it transports organs to destinations around the country.

Strata Critical Medical will continue to rely on existing agreements with aircraft operators for dedicated medical transportation. In addition to some corporate efficiencies, this should transform Strata into being a more attractive growth play for shareholders over time.

The medical logistics business segment had grown from approximately 12% of overall revenues in 2020 to a current 60%. It also accounts for roughly 85% of adjusted EBITDA for the previously combined company. Management provided initial FY2025 sales guidance of $180 million to $190 million for the remaining business.

Then in mid-September, Strata announced the purchase of Keystone Perfusion Services for $124 million (roughly nine times EBITDA). The deal was for 88% cash and 12% stock. Keystone is a major player in organ recovery and regional perfusion services within the transplant sector. Keystone should deliver 50% sales growth in FY2025 to $65 million with adjusted EBITDA of $13 million. The equity now trades for $4.25 a share and sports a market capitalization of just north $380 million.

Third Quarter Results:

Strata Critical Medical posted Q3 results on Nov. 10. The company had a GAAP loss of 12 cents a share, a dime a share below expectations. GAAP net loss for the quarter of $9.7 million, compared to $5.6 million in the same quarter a year ago. The increase was partly due to integration costs associated with the recent Keystone acquisition. The bigger impact was driven by a $5.2 million increase in a realized loss from sales of short-term investments and a legal reserve in the current period. Adjusted EBITDA rose nearly 94% to $7.6 million. Not including Keystone, this metric rose 80%.

Revenues rose nearly 37% on a year-over-year basis to $49.3 million. This beat the consensus by north of $5 million. Standalone revenue growth was 29%, which doesn't incorporate the contribution from Keystone. Management boosted FY2025 sales guidance by $5 million to $185 million to $195 million.

Analyst Commentary and Balance Sheet:

Strata Critical Medical has a solid balance sheet with no debt and just over $75 million in cash and marketable securities based on the 10-K filing for Q3.

Just under 3% of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. Strata Critical Medical has a small Wall Street following. Only four analyst firms have chimed in around Strata so far this year. That said, they're uniformly bullish, as can be seen below.

Risks/Rewards off New Business Model:

Management's embrace of a new business model has notable benefits as well as a couple of risks. By moving to an "asset lite" model, Strata reduces its capital expenditure needs substantially as well as other costs such as staffing and maintenance associated with operating and maintaining a fleet of aircraft for passenger transportation purposes. That business has been sold to Joby, as previously noted.

This should have no impact on customer relationships as far as it pertains to organ transport. Strata will remain a key player in the organ transplant ecosystem, concentrating on the organ transport logistics part of the equation, as well as now being able to offer perfusion services to customers.

Strata should continue to benefit from trends in organ transplant transportation (above), which favor larger players compared to local operations and non-profits. Key reasons this part of the business had quintupled its contribution to Blade's overall revenues since 2020, before the recent business divestiture.

A key risk is ensuring the perfusion business recently acquired by the purchase of Keystone is seamlessly integrated into Strata's overall operational processes and overall offerings. Leadership raising FY2025 guidance after Q3 results and producing operational cash flow on this front during the quarter is encouraging. Management has also stated it expects the company to consistently generate free cash flow moving forward.

The other risk is it might take a few quarters of operations under the new restructure to produce the necessary data points for investors and analyst firms to become more enthusiastic about Strata's new business model as well as its stock. Currently the company has little coverage on Wall Street, hopefully something that changes in the coming year.

Conclusion:

The one analyst firm that has posted projections sees revenues falling 23% this year with the divestiture of the passenger business. The two analyst firms that have projections out for FY2026 see sales moving to $260 million in FY2026. No analyst firms seemed to have put a target on earnings at this time.

SRTA is currently trading at under 1.5x FY2026E revenues, considerably cheaper accounting for the net cash on the company's balance sheet. Much larger TransMedics Group, Inc. (TMDX), which is also involved in the organ transplant sector, is valued at approximately 5.4x FY2026E sales.

It's admittedly an imperfect comparison. However, it does point to the potential of the shares in a growing industry. Therefore, the shares merit a small speculative holding while awaiting further data points as Strata Critical Medical embarks on its new journey.

data.title: Globant: Core Business Demand To Recover In 2026, AI-Led Inflection Ahead
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The IT Services spending slowdown crushed Globant S.A. (GLOB) share price since January by 73%, however demand is likely to re-accelerate in 2026 and the entire IT industry to benefit from the AI boom with some delay. Gartner forecasts the global IT spending to reach $ 7 trillion by 2028, adding $500 billion of projects every year.

The market is sleeping on Globant’s progressive transition from traditional IT services business model to higher growth segments such as Data Center Systems and Software. Globant is well positioned for a rebound in new verticals such as AI Pods, AI Studios, and Globant Enterprise AI platform. The company is also moving to a subscription value added revenue model that I think the market does not recognize just yet.

Not every company will be a Generative AI expert it that will take some time and budget to integrate to every business model. Globant’s backlog of high potential 100-squared accounts in industries like energy, sports and gaming looks promising in that regard but still has to be converted in to revenues.

I believe price action does not align with the long-term fundamentals. Globant is currently trading at a 15-25% discount compared to its trading peers but is at a deep discount compared to its intrinsic value should the growth re-accelerate in the next 2-3 years.

What does the Company Actually do?

Globant at its core is an IT Services player, helping other companies to design, build and scale digital products, mobile apps and platforms. Usually, the company’s consultants are helping to modernize a legacy system or create a new digital product with some software engineering involved. Today it differentiates itself in a somewhat commoditized and cyclical industry by providing high value-added services related to Cloud technologies and AI. At any rate, Globant revenues are directly linked to CIO's and CTO's IT available budgets.

Globant organizes itself by “Studios” which allows them to cover different technologies such as AI, Cloud, Gaming, Data & Analytics, Cybersecurity and so on. It gives them a vertical expertise with an some horizontal integration.

The Share Price Evolution

Globant share price stopped falling and has been consolidating since September 2025, forming a double bottom from what I can see on the chart above. It is now 10% off the lows which is an important criterion for deep value investing that I apply to myself. However, I would never invest on this alone as for a share price that has corrected 72.2% YTD. For such sharp downward moves, there is a high chance that the company is in big trouble. To complement that view, I look at the broader industry performance represented in the chart below.

We can see that the entire sector corrected on softening demand and delayed IT spending. The market worry is that the slowing growth combined with high a fixed costs base would squeeze the margins. However, it is important to note that the IT spending is highly cyclical and tied to corporate confidence. There is nothing wrong with the company nor the industry in cases like this one.

I then look for further confirmation from the brokers reports and we can see that the analyst enthusiasm has been muted so far, most of the ratings being “Hold” over the past 13 weeks. On November 14th, a J.P. Morgan analyst provided a "Hold" recommendation with a target price of $75 which is above the current share price. On the more positive side, Mayank Tandon from Needham assigned a “Buy” rating (still in November 2025) providing a $80 target price. I summarized analyst ratings in the table below and conclude that they provide about a 20-30% upside from the current share price of $61.61.

However, I find that the large brokerages are most of the time very conservative in their price targets. Usually they prefer posting +10-20% upside and re-rate the company going forward instead of making bold predictions. What is important to me is that there are no clear “Sell” rating from analysts in the most recent history which is shown in the table below. Therefore, it further confirms that the company’s business model is not broken and might be caught up in a cyclical down trend, especially if it is still generating good cash flow.

Corporate IT Demand May Rebound in 2026

The industry might be experiencing a slight slowdown, but in my opinion it is no in a structural decline. According to the Gartner study, the worldwide total IT spending is expected to grow +10% in 2025 (revised upwards) reaching $5.74 trillion, then +9.8% in 2026, exceeding $ 6 trillion.

Now if we focus only on IT Services segment which includes all services related to consulting, engineering and managed services and if we compare October 2024 and 2025 data tables I sourced from Gartner below, we see that 2025 growth has been revised downwards from +9.4% to +6.5%. Which might explain the current market pessimism for the sector. For 2026 however, IT Services growth is expected to be reach +8.7% and this might be finally an inflection point for Globant.

If Globant can position itself in Data Center segment working through migration to GPU-optimized cloud architectures or market itself as a key AI governance and enterprise integration player, the growth might be substantially higher. For example, looking at Gartner tables again, 2025 growth in Data Center has been updated from +15.5% to +46.8% on the back of by AI-related projects, including email and authoring that companies implemented.

Software spending remains strong with revised 2025 forecasts as well from +11.9% to +15.2%. Modernizing legacy apps, where Globant helps companies to migrate from old systems towards modular and could-native AI-enabled architectures is a massive opportunity for Globant that will allow it to differentiate against commodity players such as Tecsys, Inc. (TCS:CA), Cognizant Technology Solutions Corporation (CTSH), and Wipro Limited (WIT). This type of work requires software engineering depth, product design and UX skills – areas part of Globant’s DNA.

Overall, I find that the Globant business model is not broken, it is more in a transition phase and the IT industry is expected to grow significantly over the next years if there is no recession. At this point I conclude that market pessimism about Globant might be overdone, especially as historically the company has outperformed peers during the growth phases due to its premium positioning.

Q3 Results and Projected Financials

Q3 2025 results beat low expectations. Indeed, Globant reveals that is has achieved a stable top-line and cash flow performance. YoY revenue growth was up +0.4%, with a forecasted 5.8% decline for the next quarter. The conservative guidance of +1.3% revenue growth for 2025 is related to soft demand and slower ramp-up in AI business segments.

Revenue remains balanced across geographies (54% North America and 20% Latin America) and clients. Top five customers, and top ten customers represented 8.8%, 23.7%, and 33.3% of revenues as shown in the Q3 2025 report page below which I believe limits the concentration risk.

During its Q3 2025 earnings call, Globant reassured investors that the adjusted profit operations margin remains stable around 15.5%, suggesting that the company remains cost disciplined. Balance sheet is overall healthy with net leverage being 0.5x 2024 EBITDA. As margins remain stable, cash flow is positive and net debt is moderate, I conclude that there is no immediate financial risk for the company.

The Current Valuation Provides a Significant Upside

First step of the valuation process for me is to look at the closest Globant’s listed peers and to see where they are trading in terms of P/E and EV/EBITDA ratios. It is an initial benchmark which allows me to assess if the company is trading higher or lower against competition. To that end, I computed the table below with current trading multiples of the competitors such as Capgemini SE (OTCPK:CAPMF), Accenture plc (ANC), Endava plc (DAVA) and EPAM Systems, Inc. (EPAM) that I gathered in the table below. We can say that while Globant is growing faster with better profitability than the average of competitors it is also trading cheaper. It trades at a discount in terms of forward P/E ratio (23% discount) and EV/EBITDA (13%). It seems that here the market is over-penalizing Globant, because a few soft quarters of earnings and its FX volatility due to Latin America exposure. In my opinion, the extreme sell-off and prolonged bearishness does not account Globant’s transition to an AI-centric player just yet.

Trading comps valuation gives you the current status, but I am also interested in finding Globant’s intrinsic value based on its projected cash flows. In the DCF analysis below I make reasonable growth revenue assumptions, suggesting a +1.3% growth in 2025 as per the management guidance in the Q3 2025 report, then +2.5% in 2026 as I assume continued softness in IT consulting and a slow scaling of the $3.7 billion pipeline of AI projects and subscription model. This is well below Gartner projections of +8.7%. Then I assume +7.0% in 2027 due to AI projects ramp-up, pricing stability and Latin America recovery, then I assume IT Services market growth of +8.5% in 2028 and +9% in 2029. Thereafter as I assume a terminal growth rate of +3.0%, revenue growth decelerates arithmetically from +9% in 2029 to +3% in 2034. The long projections timeframe of the DCF is here to make sure it captures the full business cycle for Globant.

I assume EBITDA and EBIT margins stable respectively around 20% and 15%. Capex is 5% of revenues and effective tax rate is 22.4%. Net Working capital stays flat at 10% of revenues each year. Aligning with the fact that in consider the WACC to be 9.6% my DCF analysis yields an implied share price of $190, which represents a +210% upside vs. current levels. Implied 2025 EV/EBITDA stands at 17.7x which is reasonable compared to 24.0x company’s 5 years average.

Now what if the growth less than expected in 2034? What if the EBITDA margins are also take a hit? I covered different scenarios in the sensitivity table below. In the extreme example of +1.0% revenue growth in 2034 and 17.2% EBITDA margin, intrinsic value is still +164% higher than the current share price. I believe the worst-case scenario is already priced-in for Globant. The company is providing good value from here should the growth re-accelerate in the near-terms. A better than expected Q4 could be a good catalyst.

Probable Catalysts for Globant

There are other catalysts for Globant re-rating that include a faster than expected recovery in IT spending, accelerated adoption of the AI-driven services. This would be a net positive both for revenue growth and margin expansion with better AI Pods monetization. A faster than usual conversion into revenues from the $ 3.7 billion pipeline (+30% YoY) would come and shake my conservative projections (as well as muted analyst consensus) for 2026. Such event would undoubtably constitute a near-term bullish outlook for the share price, but still, this is not my base case here. FY2025 +1.3% revenue growth is largely driven by delays in conversion, not due to a lack of demand as the pipeline growth is 30x higher than the expected revenue.

Another important point for me to be more bullish near-term is to monitor the utilization rate of the workforce. The management mentioned in the Q3 2025 publication that it still has “room to improve”. In my view, this is more related to a temporary softness in demand rather than a structural issue. To address this Globant initiated the “Business Optimization Program” in April 2025, reducing the headcount and closing offices.

There are Still Important Risks Involved

Despite strong pipeline, the conversion of the backlog into revenues might be slow and client budgets can still be constrained for some time. IT budgets in media, travel and retail can be always volatile from one year to another.

The scale and the pace of the revenue conversion remains a concern for the broader market. In such competitive market, the company is not returning to double digit growth by miracle. This will require some time for the business model to shift and to bear its fruits. It comes with an execution risk and perhaps some margins compression in the near term.

Accenture, EPAM, ThoughtWorks, Cognizant, TCS, and even cloud vendors like Amazon Web Services (AWS) by Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Google by Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Microsoft by Corporation (MSFT) are aggressively expanding AI solutions. This is why Globant should fight for its corner by differentiating itself to defend its pricing.

Inflation and FX risks remain in countries where Globant is present especially in Argentina, Colombia and Mexico.

Investment Decision

Overall, I believe Globant is well positioned for AI integration consulting work in its client base but faces near-term headwinds in demand due to constrained IT budgets.

However, the market pessimism about Globant is such that it makes little sense for me given the fact demand in IT Services is expected to pick up in 2026. At these price levels offers an attractive deep value play for long-term investors with 2-3 years horizon. My financial projections suggest a slow cyclical recovery and return to revenue growth above +5% not less in 2 years. But even in such case, the intrinsic value of the company against the current share price is +210% higher.

data.title: Nvidia: I Was Wrong, But I'm Not A Buyer Here
data.description:
How Did Nvidia Fare In Q3 FY2026?

For fiscal Q3 2026, Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) eased past consensus estimates, with revenues coming in at $57B (+62% y/y, vs. est. $55B) and normalized EPS landing at $1.30 per share (+67% y/y, vs. est. $1.26 per share).

Now, this brute strength was driven by Nvidia's Data Center business, which clocked record revenues of $51.2B [+66% y/y, +25% q/q] powered by insatiable demand for its Blackwell GPUs:

Blackwell sales are off the charts, and cloud GPUs are sold out. Compute demand keeps accelerating and compounding across training and inference — each growing exponentially. We’ve entered the virtuous cycle of AI. The AI ecosystem is scaling fast — with more new foundation model makers, more AI startups, across more industries, and in more countries. AI is going everywhere, doing everything, all at once.”

- Jensen Huang, Nvidia's Q3 FY2026 Earnings Release (emphasis added).

Within Data Center, compute revenues rose to $43.0 billion [+56% y/y, +27% q/q] and networking revenue leapfrogged to $8.2 billion [+162% y/y, +13% q/q] based on continued growth of NVLink compute fabric for GB200 and GB300 Blackwell systems.

Now, while Nvidia's Q3 FY2026 gross margin of 73.6% was still 140 bps lower than the year-ago period, it improved by 90 bps sequentially from Q2 2025, as Blackwell ramped with an improved mix and cost structure. And this margin improvement is now projected to extend in the ongoing quarter, with Nvidia's leadership guiding for Q4 gross margin to come in at 74.8% to 75% [+/- 50 bps]:

Furthermore, Nvidia's Q4 sales guide of $65B [+/- 2%] handily exceeded pre-ER estimates of $61.84B, rendering Q3 FY2026 another triple play quarter for the semiconductor giant!

Also, Nvidia's operating cash flow expanded to $23.8B in Q3, with cash & short-term investments balance ballooning to $60.6B [despite Nvidia returning $12.7B to shareholders via buybacks and dividends during the quarter].

Now, in the immediate aftermath of the report, Nvidia's stock is jumping up by ~6% to ~$198 per share. However, despite being impressed by Nvidia's quarterly results and forward guidance, I think investors must exercise patience and await a better entry point.

Why Be Cautious?

In light of Nvidia's better-than-expected Q3 FY2026 report and robust Q4 guide, I am lifting our valuation model's revenue base from $200B FY2026E to $300B FY2027E for today's valuation exercise, building a forward-looking model. Furthermore, I am underwriting (faster, above-consensus) 20% CAGR sales growth for the 2027-2031 5-year period, emboldened by the "virtuous AI cycle" that points to a $1T+ TAM for the likes of Nvidia and AMD by 2030, ignoring all the concerns around "vendor financing and revenue roundtripping" in the AI infrastructure space.

All other assumptions have been held constant from our last assessment and are self-explanatory, but if you have any questions, please share them in the comments section below.

Here's my updated valuation model for Nvidia:

Despite modeling NVDA stock optimistically for above-consensus growth based on forward revenue numbers, I see -17% downside to fair value from current levels. Furthermore, NVDA's expected 5-year CAGR return of ~12% (derived using a premium exit multiple of ~25x P/FCF), continues to fall short of TQI's investment hurdle rate.

Final Thoughts

Amid all the "AI bubble" talk, Nvidia continues exhibiting top-line hypergrowth, whilst raking in an absolute profit windfall as of Q3 FY2026. Now, while the AI economy is becoming increasingly circular with each major AI deal announcement, Nvidia's business is in fine fettle and looks primed for robust growth in upcoming quarters, as projected AI CAPEX spending from hyperscalers, AI startups, and foreign governments is expanding rapidly.

Over the past year, I have maintained a Hold rating on NVDA stock:

Now, with Nvidia stock rising ~35% YTD and handily outperforming broader indices, I was clearly wrong to avoid NVDA.

That said, even after underwriting NVDA stock with aggressive, above-consensus future growth and margin assumptions based on forward-year revenues, the long-term risk/reward fails to meet my investment hurdle rate. In my view, Nvidia's stock is running well ahead of its business.

Consequently, I am sticking to my Hold rating for Nvidia in the $190s. However, if NVDA were to slide down to the mid-$100s over the next 6-12 months without any business fundamental deterioration, I would become a buyer.

Key Takeaway: I rate Nvidia a Hold at $197 per share.

Thanks for reading, and happy investing! Please share your thoughts, questions, or concerns in the comments section below.

data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market: Differentiated Grocer, Mispriced Stock - Buy
data.description:
Executive Summary

Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc. (SFM) is a relatively small U.S. grocery chain that focuses on natural, organic and health-oriented products sold at fair prices. Even though it operates only a bit over 440 stores in a ~$1 trillion, highly fragmented grocery market, Sprouts generates gross, operating margins and returns on invested capital that sit at the high end of its peer group. A simple ROIC breakdown shows that most of this advantage comes from higher NOPAT margins, not from unusually high asset turnover.

That points to a moat built on being different rather than being the cheapest: a trusted brand, a produce-first store layout, direct relationships with growers and a growing private-label mix that offers good value-for-money while still supporting strong profitability. That supported by underlying trends like comparable-store sales, growing margins, and supplier-related metrics have been moving in the right direction, and both a P/E–ROIC regression and a DCF framework suggest that the stock is still mostly undervalued. In my view, this supports a clear Buy.

Background

Sprouts Farmers Market is a grocery chain known for its organic, natural produce and a broad health-and-wellness assortment, offered at fair prices. The chain began as a fresh-fruit stand in California in the early 1940s, with the goal of delivering fresh produce at low prices. That goal has remained in place to this day, as reflected in how the company describes its target customer in its 10-K:

We are focusing attention on our target customers, identified through research as ‘health enthusiasts’ and ‘selective shoppers’, where there is ample opportunity to gain share within these customer segments

The fact that the company is providing us with who their customers are is important, as it helps us understand the durability and the resilience of their business. While Sprouts doesn't provide granular information regarding their customers in its earnings call or presentation, it does mention it targets higher-income customers. We can find more detail in this Grocery Dive article, which discloses the median income of Sprouts customers is $92K, compared to $76K of the general grocery. That figure is good, and we can suggest that the company's customers are somewhat resilient to the weakening economy, but not immune. While projecting what would happen with the economy is hard, some of the Bear case assumptions take into consideration a rough economic environment.

Ever since that one stand, Sprouts has grown its store base to more than 440 locations, most of which are in California (149), Texas (54), and Arizona and Florida (47 each), according to its 2024 10-K.

While ~450 stores might sound like a big number, once you compare it to the major grocery chains, you realize how small it really is. Sprouts’ footprint is basically a drop in the ocean.

On the other hand, comparing Sprouts to big-box retailers like Walmart Inc. (WMT) or Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) is kind of misleading, as Sprouts mostly resembles concepts like Whole Foods or Trader Joe’s — "healthy" groceries. Unfortunately, finding detailed financials are harder to find for these two. Trader Joe's is private and Whole Foods is a subsidiary of Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN), so a true apples to apples comparison is difficult. Difficult, but not impossible. Among public comparables, a comparison could be made with The Kroger Co. (KR) and its organic and private label brands.

The Industry

Sprouts is operating in a stable, well-researched industry - the US Retail Grocery industry. On a yearly basis, the US Retail Grocery industry is selling around $1 trillion a year, and it's highly fragmented. It is estimated to be more than 45 thousand supermarkets in the US.

Not surprisingly, the market share (by sales) of the chains kind of resembles the picture arising from the number of stores, with Walmart capturing almost a quarter (!!) of the total grocery sales in the US.

Walmart’s success is mostly driven by its pricing strategy, which builds a moat around cost advantage (i.e., Walmart can sell goods at lower prices than competitors). Its strategy is based on Every Day Low Price (or EDLP), meaning customers don’t need to wait for discounts or special promotions; instead, Walmart aims to offer the lowest possible prices every day. To do this at scale, and in a sustainable way, Walmart had to build a highly efficient, robust supply chain (which is why I’d argue Walmart is more of a logistics company than a retailer). Kroger is operating bit differently, by offering coupons and discounts regularly to compete on prices as well.

There’s another way to win in this industry: instead of competing on cost, you compete by being different. By selling differentiated products, a company can offer unique goods with a higher value proposition for its customers, and in turn support higher margins. Compared to giants like Walmart and Kroger, Sprouts under CEO Jack Sinclair has shifted away from a promotion-heavy model toward differentiation through unique non-perishable offerings, while limiting discounts and coupons mostly to rewards programs. That way, the company isn’t constantly diluting its brand and can keep that differentiated positioning in the eyes of its customers.

So, where does Sprouts fall on that scale? First, let's have a look at the margins in the industry (of the public companies). Below, we can see that Sprouts' gross and operational margins are higher than average and among the highest in the peer group.

While not public companies, the estimates for Trader Joe's and Whole Foods gross margins are in the range of mid-20s percent, and low-30s, respectively.

To complete the comparison, let's have a look at the return on invested capital (or ROIC) as well, which measures what is the yield of every $1 invested capital by the company.

Here as well, we can see that Sprouts' ROIC is way above the average and among the best in the peer group. And as shown below, this is not an anomaly, or one-time, Sprouts' ROIC is higher than average persistently, and growing over time.

So, does Sprouts' ROIC point to a moat and if it does, what is the moat Sprouts holds? To answer that question, I decomposed the ROIC into two parts. Essentially, ROIC is calculated by dividing NOPAT (Net Operating Profit After Tax) by the total invested capital of the company. We can decompose it further into the following:

ROIC = (Sales/Invested Capital) x (NOPAT/Sales). The first argument is called the invested capital turnover, which tells us how what is the amount of sales for each $1 of invested capital, and the second argument, which is the NOPAT margin, tells us what is the percentage of NOPAT for each $1 of sales.

The invested capital turnover multiplied by the NOPAT margin would eliminate the sales component for each side of the equation, and we would be left with NOPAT/Invested capital.

If a company’s invested capital turnover is high, it implies that sales are high relative to the capital invested, which increases the likelihood that the company has a cost advantage. If the NOPAT margin is high, it suggests the company is able to sell at higher prices and has a more differentiated offering for its customers.

It is not a surprise that Walmart and Costco are high on the invested capital turnover end, and as we thought, Sprouts would lie toward the end of higher NOPAT margin, which again, implies that it has a moat by being different.

Sprouts' Moat

As suggested by the ROIC decomposition, Sprouts' moat might be in their unique offering to their customers. So, let's examine that. In their 2024 10K, the company says that:

We are a specialty natural and organic food retailer offering a unique shopping experience for our customers. To offer the right assortment of healthy alternatives and good-for-you options, we curate our product mix to attribute-driven and differentiated fresh, natural and organic foods and healthier options throughout all of our departments, with innovative products that feature lifestyle friendly ingredients.

So, even the company "admits" that it tries to differentiate itself from other stores by offering unique, healthy food for its customers, which the company labels as "health enthusiasts" and "selective shoppers/experience seekers". Meaning, the company is focusing on a narrower segment of the total grocery market. According to Sprouts' CEO, Jack Sinclair, that segment is spending around $200 billion, approximately 20% of the total grocery sales.

The company attracts customers because it has built a trusted brand, as people know they’ll find those unique products at Sprouts’ stores. But how did Sprouts build that trust in the first place? By staying loyal to its core offering from that very first stand: fresh, organic produce.

And these are not "just words", it is part of their mission statement, as they write:

True to our farm-stand heritage, Sprouts offers a unique grocery experience featuring an open layout with fresh produce at the heart of the store.

And when they say the fresh produce is in the "heart of the store", they literally mean it, as Sprouts' stores are organized differently from other grocery stores.

The logic behind this layout is clear: the customer knows that the produce at Sprouts is high in quality and valuable for the price. The company is not low-priced, but offers value-for-money with the quality.

Unfortunately, there is no data on repeat customers to assess whether their strategy is working, but we can use their comparable stores' sales growth (i.e., the growth of sales in stores that have been operating for the last full five quarters) as a measure of their strategy's success.

Taking out the 2021 anomaly driven by Covid-19, we see a gradual and persistent trend of growing comparable store sales since 2019. It’s also important to note that Jack Sinclair, the CEO, started on June 24 of that year.

So, Sprouts is developing a moat around its brand, as customer knows they can always find what they are looking for, and the company is able to protect that moat by offering value-for-money with produce products. But how does it work exactly? Through the company's own private label, which has a differentiated assortment, as the CEO has said in an interview last March. In 2019, Sprouts' sales of their private label were around 14%, and in 2024, it was 24%, a significant increase. For comparison, Costco has its Kirkland Signature private label, and it's around 33% of its sales. And we can see it in Sprouts' margins.

Comparing Sprouts to Trader Joe’s and Whole Foods, each of these companies is trying to do something slightly different with its private label. Trader Joe’s leans into quirky, one-of-a-kind products that aren’t always necessarily health-focused, such as prepared meals, unique cookies and so on. Whole Foods, on the other hand, pushes its own-branded everyday items at relatively low prices. Sprouts sits somewhere else on that spectrum. As we’ve seen, its private label is much more about health — vitamins, natural and organic products, supplements, etc., and it tries to offer those at competitive prices. By being health-related brand, I believe Sprouts' moat is defensible against these two competitors, given each company's different value proposition.

Going forward, Sprouts does talk about where it sees its private label sales, but I can't believe it would be much higher from these levels, or nowhere near Trader Joe's 90% estimated private label sales. During the interview, Sinclair said it can be in the range of 20-30% of sales, and I agree that's likely.

But, as long as it would remain at these levels, Sprouts definitely could keep its margins, which grew significantly and are higher compared to others in the industry, as its value of being differentiated remains.

I know what you are thinking. "So why can't the big chains offer the same produce quality, with even lower prices?", and that is what I was thinking to myself as well. I found the answer by crossing two pieces of information, one from the great Acquired episode on Trader Joe's, and from Jack Sinclair's interview mentioned above. The key is the understanding that large chains demand unity and consistency of delivered products, which prevents them from being flexible and maintaining relations with the direct suppliers (and not 3rd party large distributors). In the mentioned interview, Sinclair says:

The other thing in produce business that I think is really important is trying to work with Growers so that we take the full compliment rather than a pepper that's a little bit misshapen but as fresh as you can like the US food industry will reject that... and the reality is we can and we do play a role in trying to do long-term commitments to Growers so that they'll give us the full compliment from that field and then and to be honest with you the farmer makes more money we get a better margin and the customer gets a better price and it's better for the environment.

Produce is around 19% of Sprouts' sales, and by my calculations, it is around 25% of the company's purchases, made from direct growers. Meaning, Sprouts has to maintain good relationships with these growers, and in general with their suppliers (around 50% of purchases are from a distributor, which Sprouts buys its dry grocery and frozen products). And I think that the Days of Payables (DPO) metric tells us (Days of Payables Outstanding measures how many days it takes on average for a company to pay its accounts payable liabilities).

Again, we can see a shift since 2019, when Sinclair took the role of CEO.

To summarize, Sprouts' moat is in being different than big retail chains such as Kroger, with unique branded product offerings, by targeting a specific niche of customers. That differentiation created a trusted brand in the eyes of their customers. This moat has proven itself with higher margins, higher ROIC, and it is also seen in the ROIC decomposition chart.

Valuation

To value Sprouts, I will use two methods: a DCF and a relative multiple valuation.

For the easy part, I will start with relative multiple valuation. I chose to regress the sample P/E ratio on a TTM basis, against ROIC, as I believe in the grocery segment is a stable one, and should be judged by a company's ability to earn return on its invested capital. As shown below, SFM is clearly undervalued by the regression (with a correlation of ~70%). According to the regression, SFM should be traded on a 30x P/E multiple, which puts it on a price target of $154.8 a share, or 88% upside. That price target resides with the bull case scenario for the DCF below, so I would take it as the upper range, but more on that in a bit.

For the DCF, I used a Bull Base Bear case to get a range of maneuvers around the fair value, in case my assumptions are out of touch. I based my assumptions on several growth pillars

First, the company's number of stores growth rate, which they target at 10% a year for the next ~8 years (to get to around 1,000-1,200 stores). The company is aiming to expand into white space markets (for them) across the North-East and Mid-West, where their presence is essentially non-existent.

Second, the general trend of the population leaning toward a healthier lifestyle - According to a McKinsey survey, there is a long-term general trend of a healthier lifestyle pushed mainly by younger generations, but it is an overall trend that is expected to continue. That trend is related to many categories, of which healthier food consumption is one of them. So that means that companies such as Sprouts and Whole Foods would take more market share of this $1 trillion market.

Third, GLP-1 (weight loss) drugs, which increase the appetite (no pun intended) for healthy, fresh, and organic food - The GLP-1 drugs, which are known as the "weight-loss" drugs, are causing a declining appetite in general in patients, which makes them lose weight. But at the same time, it causes a shift in eating habits. According to an article by UC Davis, the drug causes patients to reduce their snack and other confectionery consumption, and at the same time increases their consumption of natural, fresh fruits, vegetables, and, in general, healthier food, which is exactly what Sprouts is selling. I will note that this is a general trend that is expected to benefit Sprouts and its direct competitors, such as Whole Foods, as well. The current target customers of Sprouts, as the company describes them, are not assumed to be taking these drugs, so here as well, companies such as Sprouts and Whole Foods would take more market share.

For the discount rate, I used a Weighted Average Cost of Capital (or WACC). I calculated the cost of debt using the company’s interest on leases, and the cost of equity using CAPM (for CAPM, I estimated the beta as the average unlevered beta of a peer group).

And of course, there are risks as well. The main risks are that the company won't be able to expand successfully, and copy their success it has in its core markets, which would drive down its new store sales, and eventually the average sales per store, and the growth trajectory would go lower. Or risks of shifting preferences from the company's own products, which would erode the company's differentiated status. But given Jack Sinclair's success and successful execution, I believe these risks are priced in the Bear case scenario.

Wrapping Up

To summarize, I see Sprouts Farmers Market as a strong company, with a widening moat, that suffered based on fears about slowing comparable store sales. While an important metric, I believe even more important for the company's growth metric is their opening store rate, which again, they aim for a high growth rate of 10%.

I also believe the company is holding a strong relationship with its suppliers, which allows them to maintain their moat, and continue their differentiation with their private label products, while keeping the trust their customers have in them. Thus, my rating is Buy.
